name: English IV - Analytical Writing
description: An advanced English course focused on analytical and critical writing skills, including literary analysis, rhetorical analysis, argument evaluation, and research synthesis, culminating in a capstone portfolio.

modules.title: Analytical Thinking & Reading
modules.objectives: Develop critical thinking skills by questioning assumptions, identifying bias, and evaluating arguments in texts.
modules.readingNote: Read about critical reading and analytical thinking strategies.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on critical thinking and reading.
modules.practiceNote: Complete critical thinking exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Choose an editorial article and write a one-page analysis identifying the author's assumptions, evidence quality, and logical reasoning.

modules.title: Close Reading
modules.objectives: Apply close reading strategies to analyze texts in detail, examining word choice, structure, and tone.
modules.readingNote: Read about close reading techniques for literary texts.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on close reading strategies.
modules.practiceNote: Complete a close reading exercise on CommonLit.
modules.assignment: Select a passage of at least 300 words and write a detailed close reading annotation identifying literary devices, tone shifts, and authorial choices.

modules.title: Thesis Development
modules.objectives: Craft strong, arguable, and specific thesis statements that guide analytical essays.
modules.readingNote: Read about developing strong thesis statements.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on thesis development.
modules.practiceNote: Practice writing and revising thesis statements.
modules.assignment: Write five different thesis statements on the same topic, ranging from weak to strong, and explain what makes each stronger than the last.

modules.title: Literary Analysis
modules.objectives: Analyze literary works by examining theme, symbolism, characterization, and author's craft to develop a supported argument.
modules.readingNote: Study the techniques for writing literary analysis essays.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on literary analysis writing.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the literary analysis practice on CommonLit.
modules.assignment: Write a 5-paragraph literary analysis essay on a short story, developing an original thesis and supporting it with textual evidence.

modules.title: Textual Evidence
modules.objectives: Select, integrate, and cite textual evidence effectively to support analytical claims.
modules.readingNote: Read about quoting, paraphrasing, and summarizing effectively.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on integrating textual evidence.
modules.assignment: Take three analytical claims and for each, find two pieces of textual evidence, integrate them with signal phrases, and write a follow-up analysis sentence.

modules.title: Rhetorical Analysis
modules.objectives: Analyze how authors use rhetorical strategies—ethos, pathos, logos—to achieve their purpose with a specific audience.
modules.readingNote: Study the rhetorical situation and analysis techniques.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on rhetorical analysis.
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a persuasive article for rhetorical strategies.
modules.assignment: Write a rhetorical analysis essay on a speech or article, identifying the author's use of ethos, pathos, and logos and evaluating their effectiveness.

modules.title: Argument Evaluation
modules.objectives: Identify logical fallacies, evaluate the strength of arguments, and distinguish fact from opinion.
modules.readingNote: Explore the logical fallacy reference guide.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on logical fallacies and argument evaluation.
modules.practiceNote: Complete the logical reasoning exercises on Khan Academy.
modules.assignment: Find two arguments on a controversial issue and evaluate each for logical fallacies, evidence quality, and persuasive strength.

modules.title: Source Integration
modules.objectives: Integrate multiple sources into analytical writing using signal phrases, quotation, paraphrase, and summary.
modules.readingNote: Read about the techniques of source integration.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on integrating sources in academic writing.
modules.practiceNote: Practice creating citations and integrating sources.
modules.assignment: Write two body paragraphs that each integrate at least two sources using a mix of quotation and paraphrase with proper MLA citations.

modules.title: Research Methods
modules.objectives: Apply advanced research methods including database searches, source evaluation, and annotated bibliography creation.
modules.readingNote: Read about advanced research processes and database use.
modules.videoNote: Watch the tutorial on academic database research.
modules.practiceNote: Search JSTOR for peer-reviewed sources on your research topic.
modules.assignment: Create an annotated bibliography with five sources, including a brief summary and evaluation of each source's credibility and relevance.

modules.title: Writing Across Genres
modules.objectives: Understand how purpose, audience, and genre conventions shape analytical writing in different contexts.
modules.readingNote: Read about genre conventions in academic writing.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on writing in different genres.
modules.practiceNote: Read texts in multiple genres and compare their conventions.
modules.assignment: Write the same analytical argument in three different formats: a short essay, an editorial letter, and a social media thread, adapting tone and style for each audience.

modules.title: Style & Voice
modules.objectives: Develop a distinctive analytical writing style with appropriate academic voice, precision, and clarity.
modules.readingNote: Read about word choice, style, and academic voice.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on developing academic writing style.
modules.practiceNote: Use Grammarly to analyze and improve the style of your writing.
modules.assignment: Revise a previous analytical essay to improve academic voice, eliminate passive constructions where possible, and enhance word precision.

modules.title: Revision & Editing
modules.objectives: Apply systematic revision and editing strategies to produce polished analytical essays.
modules.readingNote: Study advanced revision and editing strategies.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on revision strategies for analytical writing.
modules.practiceNote: Use Grammarly for a systematic grammar and style check.
modules.assignment: Exchange an essay with a partner, complete a structured peer review using a rubric, revise your own essay based on feedback, and submit both drafts.

modules.title: Synthesis Writing
modules.objectives: Synthesize multiple sources into a coherent analytical argument that goes beyond summarizing individual sources.
modules.videoNote: Watch the video on synthesis writing.
modules.practiceNote: Gather three sources on a topic and practice synthesizing their arguments.
modules.assignment: Write a 5-paragraph synthesis essay using four sources, demonstrating how they relate to each other and to your central thesis.

quizQuestions.question: What is analytical thinking?
options: Breaking down complex ideas to understand them critically
quizQuestions.answer: Breaking down complex ideas to understand them critically
quizQuestions.explanation: Analytical thinking involves examining ideas, identifying assumptions, evaluating evidence, and drawing reasoned conclusions.

quizQuestions.question: What does it mean to identify bias in a text?
options: Recognizing when an author presents only one-sided or subjective information
quizQuestions.answer: Recognizing when an author presents only one-sided or subjective information
quizQuestions.explanation: Identifying bias means recognizing when a text unfairly favors one perspective or omits key contrary evidence.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Analytical thinking is "Breaking down complex ideas to understand them critically".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Breaking down complex ideas to understand them critically is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is close reading?
options: Reading a text quickly for the main idea
A detailed, careful analysis of a short passage focusing on language and style
quizQuestions.answer: A detailed, careful analysis of a short passage focusing on language and style
quizQuestions.explanation: Close reading involves carefully examining the specific language, structure, and meaning of a text passage.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'diction' refer to in a close reading?
quizQuestions.answer: The author's word choice
quizQuestions.explanation: Diction refers to the particular words an author selects and how those choices contribute to meaning and tone.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'tone' in a literary text?
options: The volume at which the text is read
The author's attitude or emotional stance toward the subject
quizQuestions.answer: The author's attitude or emotional stance toward the subject
quizQuestions.explanation: Tone is the author's emotional attitude toward the subject or audience, conveyed through word choice and style.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Close reading is "A detailed, careful analysis of a short passage focusing on language and style".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A detailed, careful analysis of a short passage focusing on language and style is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What makes a thesis statement analytical rather than informational?
options: It makes a debatable claim that requires evidence and reasoning
quizQuestions.answer: It makes a debatable claim that requires evidence and reasoning
quizQuestions.explanation: An analytical thesis presents an arguable interpretation or evaluation that must be supported with evidence.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a strong analytical thesis?
options: This essay is about the theme of courage.
Shakespeare's Hamlet uses the motif of corruption to argue that unchecked ambition destroys moral integrity.
Hamlet is a play about a prince.
quizQuestions.answer: Shakespeare's Hamlet uses the motif of corruption to argue that unchecked ambition destroys moral integrity.
quizQuestions.explanation: A strong analytical thesis makes a specific, arguable claim about how a text works and what it means.

quizQuestions.question: What should a thesis statement do?
options: State the writer's main analytical argument and preview how it will be supported
List all the topics in the essay
quizQuestions.answer: State the writer's main analytical argument and preview how it will be supported
quizQuestions.explanation: A thesis presents the central argument and often signals the key points of evidence that will follow.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary goal of a literary analysis essay?
options: To argue an interpretation of a text using evidence
To retell the story in your own words
quizQuestions.answer: To argue an interpretation of a text using evidence
quizQuestions.explanation: Literary analysis essays present and defend a specific interpretation or evaluation of a literary work.

quizQuestions.question: What is a motif in literature?
options: A recurring element that has symbolic significance
quizQuestions.answer: A recurring element that has symbolic significance
quizQuestions.explanation: A motif is a recurring image, idea, or symbol in a literary work that develops theme and meaning.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'irony' mean in literary analysis?
options: A contrast between expectation and reality
quizQuestions.answer: A contrast between expectation and reality
quizQuestions.explanation: Irony occurs when there is a gap between what is said or expected and what actually happens or is meant.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 'author's craft'?
options: The physical tools the author uses
The deliberate choices an author makes about language, structure, and technique
quizQuestions.answer: The deliberate choices an author makes about language, structure, and technique
quizQuestions.explanation: Author's craft refers to the intentional literary and stylistic decisions made to create meaning and effect.

quizQuestions.question: What is a signal phrase when integrating evidence?
options: An introductory phrase that attributes a quote or idea to its source
quizQuestions.answer: An introductory phrase that attributes a quote or idea to its source
quizQuestions.explanation: Signal phrases introduce quotations or paraphrases and credit the source.

quizQuestions.question: What should follow every piece of textual evidence in an analytical essay?
options: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
quizQuestions.answer: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
quizQuestions.explanation: After providing evidence, the writer must analyze how it connects to and supports the thesis or paragraph claim.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A signal phrase when integrating evidence is "An introductory phrase that attributes a quote or idea to its source".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. An introductory phrase that attributes a quote or idea to its source is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "What should follow every piece of textual evidence in an analytical essay" is: Another quotation.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim.

quizQuestions.question: What does rhetorical analysis examine?
options: How an author uses language and strategies to persuade or affect an audience
quizQuestions.answer: How an author uses language and strategies to persuade or affect an audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Rhetorical analysis examines the techniques an author uses to achieve their purpose with a specific audience.

quizQuestions.question: What is the rhetorical situation?
options: The context including speaker, audience, purpose, and text
The emotional appeal of the text
quizQuestions.answer: The context including speaker, audience, purpose, and text
quizQuestions.explanation: The rhetorical situation encompasses the context in which communication occurs: speaker, audience, purpose, topic, and occasion.

quizQuestions.question: How does kairos function in rhetoric?
options: It is an appeal to logic
It refers to the right moment or timing for communication
quizQuestions.answer: It refers to the right moment or timing for communication
quizQuestions.explanation: Kairos refers to the opportune moment or context in which communication is most effective.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does rhetorical analysis examine" is: How an author uses language and strategies to persuade or affect an audience.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. How an author uses language and strategies to persuade or affect an audience is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is a logical fallacy?
options: An error in reasoning that undermines an argument
quizQuestions.answer: An error in reasoning that undermines an argument
quizQuestions.explanation: A logical fallacy is a flaw in reasoning that makes an argument invalid or misleading.

quizQuestions.question: What is an ad hominem fallacy?
options: Attacking the argument instead of the person
Attacking the person making the argument instead of the argument itself
quizQuestions.answer: Attacking the person making the argument instead of the argument itself
quizQuestions.explanation: Ad hominem fallacy occurs when someone attacks character rather than the argument.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: A logical fallacy is "An error in reasoning that undermines an argument".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. An error in reasoning that undermines an argument is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is the purpose of a signal phrase when integrating sources?
options: To attribute the source and introduce the evidence smoothly
quizQuestions.answer: To attribute the source and introduce the evidence smoothly
quizQuestions.explanation: Signal phrases introduce quotations or paraphrases and give credit to the author.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between summary and paraphrase?
options: Summary condenses the whole source; paraphrase restates a specific passage in your own words
quizQuestions.answer: Summary condenses the whole source; paraphrase restates a specific passage in your own words
quizQuestions.explanation: Summary provides an overview of an entire source; paraphrase restates a specific section in the writer's own words.

quizQuestions.question: What is plagiarism?
options: Using someone else's words or ideas without proper attribution
Writing in a style similar to another author
quizQuestions.answer: Using someone else's words or ideas without proper attribution
quizQuestions.explanation: Plagiarism is presenting another person's intellectual work as your own without proper citation.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The purpose of a signal phrase when integrating sources is "To attribute the source and introduce the evidence smoothly".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. To attribute the source and introduce the evidence smoothly is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is an annotated bibliography?
options: A list of sources that includes a brief summary and evaluation of each source
quizQuestions.answer: A list of sources that includes a brief summary and evaluation of each source
quizQuestions.explanation: An annotated bibliography provides citations followed by a brief paragraph summarizing and evaluating each source.

quizQuestions.question: What does it mean to evaluate a source's credibility?
options: Assessing the author's expertise, publication venue, and currency of information
quizQuestions.answer: Assessing the author's expertise, publication venue, and currency of information
quizQuestions.explanation: Evaluating credibility involves checking the author's qualifications, the publication's reputation, and how current the information is.

quizQuestions.question: What is a peer-reviewed article?
options: An article evaluated by experts in the field before publication
quizQuestions.answer: An article evaluated by experts in the field before publication
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer-reviewed articles have been reviewed and approved by other experts in the field.

quizQuestions.question: What does 'writing across genres' mean?
options: Adapting writing style, tone, and structure to fit different genres and audiences
quizQuestions.answer: Adapting writing style, tone, and structure to fit different genres and audiences
quizQuestions.explanation: Writing across genres means understanding how purpose, audience, and context require different forms of communication.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'audience awareness' in writing?
options: Knowing how many people will read your essay
Understanding who your readers are and adapting your writing to their needs and expectations
quizQuestions.answer: Understanding who your readers are and adapting your writing to their needs and expectations
quizQuestions.explanation: Audience awareness means tailoring content, tone, vocabulary, and style to the specific readers you are addressing.

quizQuestions.question: How does an editorial differ from a research essay?
options: An editorial expresses opinion for a public audience; a research essay presents evidence-based argument in an academic context
A research essay is more emotional
quizQuestions.answer: An editorial expresses opinion for a public audience; a research essay presents evidence-based argument in an academic context
quizQuestions.explanation: Editorials aim to persuade a general readership; research essays present thoroughly evidenced arguments for academic audiences.

quizQuestions.question: What is purpose in writing?
options: The writer's reason for writing and what they want to accomplish with the audience
quizQuestions.answer: The writer's reason for writing and what they want to accomplish with the audience
quizQuestions.explanation: Purpose refers to the goal the writer aims to achieve: to inform, persuade, entertain, analyze, or synthesize.

quizQuestions.question: What is academic voice in writing?
options: Writing in a very formal, impersonal tone with no opinion
A tone that is clear, precise, objective, and appropriately formal for an academic context
Using as many complex words as possible
quizQuestions.answer: A tone that is clear, precise, objective, and appropriately formal for an academic context
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic voice balances clarity, precision, and appropriate formality.

quizQuestions.question: What is the difference between active and passive voice?
options: In active voice, the subject performs the action; in passive voice, the subject receives the action
quizQuestions.answer: In active voice, the subject performs the action; in passive voice, the subject receives the action
quizQuestions.explanation: Active voice is generally clearer and more direct than passive voice.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Academic voice in writing is "A tone that is clear, precise, objective, and appropriately formal for an academic context".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A tone that is clear, precise, objective, and appropriately formal for an academic context is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The difference between active and passive voice is "Active voice is longer".
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: In active voice, the subject performs the action; in passive voice, the subject receives the action.

quizQuestions.question: What is the goal of the revision stage?
options: To improve the content, structure, and development of the essay
quizQuestions.answer: To improve the content, structure, and development of the essay
quizQuestions.explanation: Revision focuses on big-picture improvements: strengthening the thesis, developing arguments, improving organization.

quizQuestions.question: What should a peer reviewer focus on during feedback?
options: Evaluating the strength of the argument, evidence, and organization
quizQuestions.answer: Evaluating the strength of the argument, evidence, and organization
quizQuestions.explanation: Productive peer review addresses content-level concerns: thesis clarity, evidence quality, logical flow, and development.

quizQuestions.question: What is 'global revision'?
options: Revising for big-picture issues like argument, structure, and development
quizQuestions.answer: Revising for big-picture issues like argument, structure, and development
quizQuestions.explanation: Global revision addresses the overall effectiveness of the essay: the argument's logic, organization, and evidence.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The goal of the revision stage is "To improve the content, structure, and development of the essay".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. To improve the content, structure, and development of the essay is correct.

quizQuestions.question: What is synthesis writing?
options: Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop and support an original argument
Listing the main points of each source
quizQuestions.answer: Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop and support an original argument
quizQuestions.explanation: Synthesis writing goes beyond summarizing individual sources to show how multiple sources relate and connect to your own argument.

quizQuestions.question: What distinguishes synthesis from summary?
options: Synthesis connects multiple sources to your own argument; summary restates a single source
quizQuestions.answer: Synthesis connects multiple sources to your own argument; summary restates a single source
quizQuestions.explanation: Synthesis integrates multiple perspectives to support an original thesis, while summary merely restates what a source says.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: Synthesis writing is "Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop and support an original argument".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop and support an original argument is correct.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an analytical essay?
options: To develop and defend an interpretation or evaluation using evidence
questions.answer: To develop and defend an interpretation or evaluation using evidence
questions.explanation: Analytical essays present and support an original claim through critical examination of evidence.

questions.question: What is close reading?
options: A detailed analysis of a short passage examining language, structure, and meaning
questions.answer: A detailed analysis of a short passage examining language, structure, and meaning
questions.explanation: Close reading involves careful, detailed examination of a text's specific language and how it creates meaning.

questions.question: What makes a thesis analytical?
options: It makes a debatable, specific claim requiring evidence and reasoning
questions.answer: It makes a debatable, specific claim requiring evidence and reasoning
questions.explanation: An analytical thesis presents an arguable interpretation that must be defended with evidence.

questions.question: True or False: The function of a signal phrase is "To introduce and attribute a source smoothly into the text".
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. To introduce and attribute a source smoothly into the text is correct.

questions.question: What does rhetorical analysis examine?
options: The author's use of language and strategies to influence an audience
questions.answer: The author's use of language and strategies to influence an audience
questions.explanation: Rhetorical analysis examines how an author uses ethos, pathos, logos, and other strategies to achieve their purpose.

questions.question: What is an ad hominem logical fallacy?
options: Drawing a broad conclusion from little evidence
Attacking the person rather than their argument
questions.answer: Attacking the person rather than their argument
questions.explanation: Ad hominem fallacy occurs when someone attacks a person's character instead of engaging with their actual argument.

questions.question: What should follow every piece of textual evidence in an analytical paragraph?
options: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
questions.answer: Analysis explaining how the evidence supports the claim
questions.explanation: After providing evidence, the writer must analyze how it connects to and supports the paragraph's claim.

questions.question: True or False: Diction is "The sentence structure of a text".
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The author's word choice.

questions.question: What does evaluating a source's credibility involve?
options: Assessing the author's expertise, publication venue, and information currency
questions.answer: Assessing the author's expertise, publication venue, and information currency
questions.explanation: Credibility evaluation considers the author's qualifications, the publication's reputation, and the information's currency.

questions.question: What is a motif in literary analysis?
options: A recurring element that carries symbolic significance
questions.answer: A recurring element that carries symbolic significance
questions.explanation: A motif is a recurring image, idea, or symbol in a literary work that develops theme and meaning.

questions.question: What is the rhetorical situation?
options: The context including speaker, audience, purpose, and occasion
questions.answer: The context including speaker, audience, purpose, and occasion
questions.explanation: The rhetorical situation encompasses the context in which communication occurs.

questions.question: True or False: Synthesis writing is "Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop an original argument".
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Combining ideas from multiple sources to develop an original argument is correct.

questions.question: What is the CRAAP test used for?
options: Evaluating the credibility and quality of sources
questions.answer: Evaluating the credibility and quality of sources
questions.explanation: The CRAAP test evaluates sources based on Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, and Purpose.

questions.question: What is academic voice?
options: Writing in a very impersonal tone with no argument
A clear, precise, and appropriately formal tone for academic contexts
questions.answer: A clear, precise, and appropriately formal tone for academic contexts
questions.explanation: Academic voice balances clarity, precision, and appropriate formality.

questions.question: What is plagiarism?
options: Using someone else's words or ideas without proper attribution
Writing in a style similar to another author
questions.answer: Using someone else's words or ideas without proper attribution
questions.explanation: Plagiarism is presenting another person's intellectual work as your own without proper citation.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "What distinguishes global revision from local revision" is: Global is longer.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Global addresses argument and structure; local addresses sentence-level issues.

questions.question: What is a straw man fallacy?
options: Attacking the person making an argument
Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to refute
Drawing a broad conclusion from little evidence
questions.answer: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to refute
questions.explanation: A straw man fallacy distorts an opponent's position into a weaker version that is easier to attack.

questions.question: What is the capstone portfolio's purpose?
options: To showcase polished analytical work and demonstrate growth
questions.answer: To showcase polished analytical work and demonstrate growth
questions.explanation: The capstone portfolio presents the writer's best work as evidence of learning and development.

questions.question: What is quote sandwiching?
options: Introducing a quote, providing it, then analyzing its significance
Placing quotes only at the end of paragraphs
questions.answer: Introducing a quote, providing it, then analyzing its significance
questions.explanation: Quote sandwiching ensures evidence is properly framed: introduced, quoted, then analyzed.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does 'audience awareness' mean in analytical writing" is: Tailoring content, tone, and style to the specific readers you address.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Tailoring content, tone, and style to the specific readers you address is correct.

questions.question: What is an annotated bibliography?
options: A list of sources that includes a summary and evaluation of each
questions.answer: A list of sources that includes a summary and evaluation of each
questions.explanation: An annotated bibliography provides citations followed by a brief paragraph summarizing and evaluating each source.

questions.question: What is the difference between summary and synthesis?
options: Summary restates one source; synthesis integrates multiple sources to support an argument
questions.answer: Summary restates one source; synthesis integrates multiple sources to support an argument
questions.explanation: Synthesis goes beyond summarizing to show how multiple sources relate and connect to your own argument.

questions.question: What does word precision mean in analytical writing?
options: Choosing words that convey exactly the intended meaning
questions.answer: Choosing words that convey exactly the intended meaning
questions.explanation: Word precision means selecting the most accurate term to express an idea.

questions.question: True or False: A roadmap thesis is "A thesis about geography".
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: A thesis that states the argument and previews the key supporting points.

questions.question: What is irony in literary analysis?
options: A contrast between expectation and reality
questions.answer: A contrast between expectation and reality
questions.explanation: Irony occurs when there is a discrepancy between what is said or expected and what actually happens or is meant.

questions.question: What does metacognition mean in portfolio reflection?
options: Thinking about your own thinking and learning process
questions.answer: Thinking about your own thinking and learning process
questions.explanation: Metacognition is the awareness and evaluation of one's own thought processes.

questions.question: What is the ARMS revision strategy?
questions.answer: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute
questions.explanation: ARMS guides revision: Add missing content, Remove unnecessary material, Move sections for clarity, Substitute better choices.

questions.question: True or False: The difference between a primary and a secondary source is "Primary sources are original materials; secondary sources analyze or interpret primary sources".
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Primary sources are original materials; secondary sources analyze or interpret primary sources is correct.

questions.question: What is kairos in rhetoric?
options: The right moment or timing for communication
questions.answer: The right moment or timing for communication
questions.explanation: Kairos refers to the opportune moment or context in which communication is most effective.

questions.question: What is the author's craft?
options: The physical tools the author uses
The deliberate choices an author makes about language, structure, and technique
questions.answer: The deliberate choices an author makes about language, structure, and technique
questions.explanation: Author's craft refers to intentional literary and stylistic decisions made to create meaning and effect.

questions.question: What is hedging language?
options: Using cautious language when making uncertain or qualified claims
questions.answer: Using cautious language when making uncertain or qualified claims
questions.explanation: Hedging language qualifies claims appropriately when certainty is not warranted.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "What does 'writing across genres' mean" is: Writing very long essays.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Adapting style, tone, and structure to fit different genres and audiences.

questions.question: What is a hasty generalization?
options: Drawing a broad conclusion from insufficient evidence
questions.answer: Drawing a broad conclusion from insufficient evidence
questions.explanation: A hasty generalization draws a broad conclusion based on a small or unrepresentative sample.

questions.question: What is the purpose of an annotated bibliography entry?
options: To summarize the source and evaluate its credibility and relevance to the research
questions.answer: To summarize the source and evaluate its credibility and relevance to the research
questions.explanation: Each annotated bibliography entry summarizes content and evaluates the source's quality and usefulness.

questions.question: What does the writer's role in a synthesis essay involve?
options: Remaining neutral and only quoting sources
Developing and defending an original argument using sources as support
questions.answer: Developing and defending an original argument using sources as support
questions.explanation: In synthesis writing, the writer's original argument drives the essay, with sources serving as supporting evidence.
